course: ML / Algorithms — KV Cache & Dijkstra's Algorithm (NTU EE4033-01, Spring 2025, Lecture 11)

candidates.why_it_matters: KV Cache is the foundational optimization that makes LLM inference practical. Without it, every new token would require recomputing key/value projections for all past tokens, making inference cost grow quadratically with sequence length. With KV Cache, only q_t is new per step; k_1..k_{t-1} and v_1..v_{t-1} are read from memory. Every other technique covered in the lecture (MQA, GQA, MLA, sliding window, pruning) is a variant or extension of this base idea.
candidates.transcript_summary: KVcache.pdf slides 2-5 walk step-by-step: at step t=4, only q_4 is freshly computed; k_1..k_3 and v_1..v_3 are fetched from cache. Slide 5 shows the full multi-layer KV tensor layout across heads and layers. Slide 7 (Gemma 2 table) quantifies the cost: 46 layers x 32 heads x 128 head-dim x 2 bytes (FP16) x 2 (K+V) = 753664 bytes = 0.72 MB per token, so an A100 80 GB can cache ~114k tokens after model weights.

candidates.concept: KV Cache Memory Footprint and Hardware Constraints
candidates.why_it_matters: Understanding how much memory a KV cache consumes and how that limits maximum context length on a given GPU is a critical engineering skill for deploying LLMs. The concrete Gemma 2-27B calculation grounds the theory in real hardware numbers and motivates all cache-reduction techniques. The HBM-SRAM diagram shows why memory bandwidth, not FLOPs, is typically the binding constraint during decoding.
candidates.transcript_summary: Slide 6 shows the HBM (large/slow) vs. SRAM (small/fast) memory hierarchy. Slide 7 provides the per-token formula and a fully worked numeric example for Gemma 2 27B. The final summary table on slide 27 compares Flash Attention, KV Cache, MQA/GQA, MLA, Sliding Window, StreamingLLM, Pruning, and Speculative Decoding across: changes attention?, requires training?, other costs.

candidates.concept: Attention Head Variants for KV Cache Reduction: MHA vs. MQA vs. GQA vs. MLA
candidates.why_it_matters: Reducing KV cache size without degrading model quality is one of the central research problems in efficient LLM serving. The lecture traces the design progression: MHA (one KV per head) to MQA (one shared KV for all heads) to GQA (groups of heads share a KV pair; used in Llama, Gemma) to MLA (DeepSeek: compress all K/V into a low-dim latent c; cache only c; reconstruct using the identity alpha = q^T W_k c = (W_k^T q)^T c).
candidates.transcript_summary: Slides 8-9 show side-by-side diagrams of MHA, MQA, and GQA with Llama/Gemma cited as GQA users. Slides 10-15 derive MLA in detail: the absorbed-query trick for keys (slide 13) and the analogous W_v absorption for values (slide 15). Slide 12 shows per-query latent vectors c_1..c_4.

candidates.concept: KV Cache Eviction Strategies: Sliding Window Attention and StreamingLLM
candidates.why_it_matters: When context length exceeds GPU memory, the KV cache must be managed dynamically. Sliding Window Attention restricts attention to a local window, bounding memory at O(W) per layer. StreamingLLM augments the window with attention sink tokens, enabling stable infinite-length generation while maintaining near-training perplexity.
candidates.transcript_summary: Slides 16-17 compare two sliding-window implementations (Mistral 7B and GPT-OSS). Slide 18 shows PPL-vs-input-length curves for Llama-2-7B and Pythia-12B: StreamingLLM maintains flat perplexity to 20K tokens while dense and window-only attention degrade.

candidates.concept: KV Cache Pruning: Scissorhands and H2O
candidates.why_it_matters: Real attention maps are highly sparse: a minority of tokens repeatedly attract the majority of attention mass. Scissorhands and H2O evict low-importance KV entries, achieving up to 5x compression with small accuracy loss. No retraining required — only a lightweight eviction policy at inference time.
candidates.transcript_summary: Slide 19 shows sparse attention maps at three decoding positions (178, 228, 278) demonstrating consistent heavy-hitter patterns. Slide 20 shows Scissorhands compression-vs-accuracy curves on OPT-13B/30B/66B.

candidates.concept: Cross-Dialog Prompt Caching (Prefix Caching for LLM APIs)
prerequisites: LLM API basics (system prompt, tokens, cost)
candidates.why_it_matters: Prefix caching extends KV reuse across separate API calls that share a common prefix. API providers price cached tokens at ~10x less than fresh tokens. Research (arXiv 2601.06007) measures up to 79.6% cost reduction and 22.9% TTFT reduction. For AI agents that re-send a large system prompt on every tool call, placing stable content first is the key design principle.
candidates.transcript_summary: Slide 21 shows cross-dialog KV sharing. Slide 22 shows OpenAI pricing (gpt-5.4: $2.50 input vs. $0.25 cached input per 1M tokens). Slides 23-24 cover AI agent use case with AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, MEMORY.md placed first. Slide 25 illustrates prompt templating for better cache hit rate. Slide 26 shows cost and TTFT reductions across GPT-5.2, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4o.

candidates.why_it_matters: Dijkstra's algorithm is the canonical single-source shortest-path algorithm for non-negative-weight graphs, underlying navigation systems, network routing, and ML pipelines. The lecture covers pseudocode, a detailed step-by-step example, three priority-queue implementations with their time complexities, and a formal correctness proof by induction.
candidates.transcript_summary: Dijkstra.pdf slide 2 presents the algorithm alongside Prim's MST to highlight structural similarity. Slide 3 traces a 6-node example (s, t, x, y, z) through five while-loop iterations with colored nodes and predecessor arrows. Slide 4 is a practice problem on a 6-node graph (s, a, b, c, d, e). Slide 5 gives time complexity: O(V^2) with linear array, O(E log V) with binary heap, O(E + V log V) with Fibonacci heap. Slide 6 proves correctness via loop invariant (for all v in S: v.d = delta(s,v)) proved by induction on |S|.

selected_concepts: KV Cache Memory Footprint and Hardware Constraints
Attention Head Variants for KV Cache Reduction: MHA vs. MQA vs. GQA vs. MLA
Cross-Dialog Prompt Caching (Prefix Caching for LLM APIs)
selection_rationale: The five selected concepts span both source documents and form a pedagogically complete arc. KV Cache (autoregressive mechanism) is the foundational concept — all other KV optimizations build on it and it admits a compelling before/after timing demo in pure PyTorch. Memory Footprint Analysis provides an interactive calculation cell where students manipulate real model hyperparameters. Attention Head Variants (MHA/MQA/GQA/MLA) are the most research-active sub-topic and can be demonstrated with memory-measurement cells using toy models. Cross-Dialog Prompt Caching connects hardware intuition to real API engineering practice with measurable cost/latency outcomes. Dijkstra's Algorithm is the core concept from the Algorithms lecture and has a rich step-by-step visualization opportunity plus interactive exercises. KV Cache eviction strategies (Sliding Window, StreamingLLM, Pruning) were de-prioritized because those demos require loading actual checkpoint weights and are noted as optional extension cells.
assumptions: Both PDFs were fully read directly: KVcahce.pdf (27 slides) and Dijkstra.pdf (6 slides, NTU EE4033-01 Lecture 11, Spring 2025, instructor Shao-Hua Sun).
No instructor transcript was provided; all concept extraction is based directly on slide content.
Target audience: NTU EE upper-level undergraduates or graduate students with prior exposure to Transformer attention equations, basic graph algorithms, and Python.
Notebook environment: Google Colab with a standard GPU runtime (T4 or better); no custom CUDA kernels required.
For the Prompt Caching demo, an Anthropic or OpenAI API key is assumed available.
Gemma 2 27B (46 layers, 32 heads, 128 head-dim, FP16) is used as the concrete arithmetic reference; actual model weight loading is not required.
